abstract: Controlling the surface strain—the compression or expansion of surface atoms—is a powerful strategy for tuning the chemistry and catalytic efficiency of nanoparticles (NPs). In this work, we demonstrate how the surface platinum (Pt) strain in core/shell FePt/Pt NPs, featuring a three-atomic-layer Pt shell, can be precisely modulated by inducing a structural transition in the core from a cubic solid solution (fcc) to a tetragonal intermetallic (fct) framework. Our findings show that fct-FePt/Pt NPs exhibit significantly enhanced activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Quantum mechanics–molecular mechanics (QM–MM) simulations suggest that this performance boost stems from the fct core’s ability to relieve the overcompressed strain within the Pt shell. We further optimized this effect by partially substituting Fe with Cu to form fct-FeCuPt/Pt NPs. These trimetallic catalysts emerged as the most efficient for ORR, delivering a specific activity nearly 10 times higher than that of luxury-grade commercial Pt catalysts. This structure-driven control over surface strain provides a robust new blueprint for designing and optimizing nanoparticle catalysts for ORR and a wide range of other chemical applications.
